title: How should you store user passwords?
prompt_md: Your app needs to store user passwords so people can log in later. What is the correct way to store them?
options: Encrypted with a secret key, so you can decrypt and compare the original later
Hashed with a slow, salted password hash (bcrypt, argon2, or PBKDF2) — store only the hash, never anything reversible
Hashed once with fast MD5 and no salt, which is plenty for passwords
As plaintext, so you can email people their password if they forget it
explanation_md: Store a **one-way hash** produced by a purpose-built password hashing algorithm — **bcrypt**, **argon2**, or **PBKDF2** — with a unique **salt** per user. At login you hash the attempt and compare hashes; you never need the original. Plaintext is catastrophic if the DB leaks. Reversible **encryption** is nearly as bad: anyone with the key recovers every password. Fast unsalted hashes like MD5 are crackable in bulk with rainbow tables and brute force. The whole point of bcrypt/argon2/PBKDF2 is that they're *deliberately slow and salted*, which barely affects one honest login but cripples mass guessing.
